articles.title: Paleontology rocked by discovery of organic molecules in 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered compelling evidence that dinosaur fossils may still contain traces of their original proteins, overturning a long-standing belief that fossilization destroys all organic material. In a remarkably well-preserved Edmontosaurus fossil from South Dakota, researchers detected remnants of collagen — the main protein found in bone — using advanced techniques including mass spectrometry and protein sequencing.
articles.content:
For decades, scientists believed dinosaur fossils were little more than mineralized rock, with any original biological material long since destroyed by time. But an extraordinary study centered on a remarkably preserved Edmontosaurus fossil is challenging that assumption in a major way.

Researchers led by the University of Liverpool uncovered strong evidence that traces of original organic molecules, including collagen, still exist inside dinosaur bones dating back roughly 66 million years. The discovery adds powerful new support to a controversial idea that has divided paleontologists for more than 30 years.

Preserved Collagen Found in Dinosaur Bone

The fossil at the center of the study is a 22-kilogram Edmontosaurus sacrum, part of the dinosaur's hip region, recovered from South Dakota's famous Hell Creek Formation. Edmontosaurus was a large duck-billed plant eater that lived alongside Tyrannosaurus rex near the end of the Cretaceous Period.

Using a combination of advanced laboratory methods, including protein sequencing and several forms of mass spectrometry, scientists detected remnants of collagen embedded within the fossilized bone. Collagen is the primary structural protein found in bone tissue and one of the hardest biomolecules to explain away as contamination when identified in this context.

Researchers from UCLA also identified hydroxyproline, an amino acid strongly associated with collagen in bone. According to the team, this represented an important confirmation that degraded collagen fragments were genuinely present inside the fossil.

Professor Steve Taylor, chair of the Mass Spectrometry Research Group at the University of Liverpool's Department of Electrical Engineering & Electronics, said:

"This research shows beyond doubt that organic biomolecules, such as proteins like collagen, appear to be present in some fossils."

"Our results have far-reaching implications. Firstly, it refutes the hypothesis that any organics found in fossils must result from contamination."

A Debate That Has Divided Paleontology

Claims of preserved soft tissues and proteins in dinosaur fossils have sparked fierce debate since the early 2000s. Some scientists argued the reported materials were modern contamination or bacterial residue rather than authentic dinosaur molecules.

One of the most famous discoveries came in 2005, when paleontologist Mary Schweitzer and colleagues reported soft tissue structures inside a Tyrannosaurus rex fossil. Later studies identified possible collagen and blood vessel-like structures in additional dinosaur specimens, including hadrosaurs related to Edmontosaurus.

The new Edmontosaurus analysis stands out because researchers used multiple independent testing methods to examine the same fossil. By combining microscopy, chemical analysis, and protein sequencing, the team aimed to rule out contamination and strengthen the case that the molecules were original to the dinosaur itself.

The findings were published in Analytical Chemistry in 2025 under the title "Evidence for Endogenous Collagen in Edmontosaurus Fossil Bone."

Why This Discovery Matters

If proteins can survive in fossils for tens of millions of years, scientists may gain an entirely new way to study extinct animals.

Tiny molecular traces could potentially reveal evolutionary relationships between dinosaur species that are difficult to identify from bones alone. Researchers may also learn more about dinosaur growth, aging, physiology, and disease.

Taylor noted that scientists may now need to revisit fossil samples collected over the past century. Cross-polarized light microscopy images taken decades ago could contain overlooked evidence of preserved collagen in ancient bones.

"These images may reveal intact patches of bone collagen, potentially offering a ready-made trove of fossil candidates for further protein analysis," Taylor explained.

"This could unlock new insights into dinosaurs, for example revealing connections between dinosaur species that remain unknown."

The Mystery of Molecular Survival

The discovery also raises a fascinating scientific question: how did these molecules survive for so long?

Proteins normally break down over time, especially across geological timescales. Yet some fossils appear capable of preserving microscopic biological structures under specific conditions.

Scientists are increasingly investigating whether mineral interactions inside bone may help shield fragments of collagen from complete decay. Recent studies exploring fossil biomolecules suggest that certain burial environments and microscopic bone structures may create stable conditions that slow chemical breakdown dramatically.

Edmontosaurus fossils are already famous for their exceptional preservation. Some specimens discovered over the last century retained detailed skin impressions and other soft tissue features, earning the nickname "dinosaur mummies."

More recent paleontology research has continued uncovering surprisingly detailed soft tissue preservation in Edmontosaurus specimens, including evidence of fleshy structures and preserved skin anatomy.

Together, these discoveries are reshaping how scientists think about fossils. Instead of viewing them solely as stone replicas of ancient bones, researchers are beginning to see some fossils as possible molecular time capsules that still preserve traces of prehistoric biology millions of years later.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of preserved organic molecules, including collagen, in 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones represents a major scientific breakthrough with broad implications for paleontology and evolutionary biology. It challenges long-standing assumptions about fossilization, opens new avenues for studying extinct species at a molecular level, and could transform how fossils are analyzed worldwide. The article provides detailed context, methodology, and significance, demonstrating substantial real-world impact and scientific advancement.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find ancient proteins inside 66-million-year-old dinosaur bones
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, scientists thought that dinosaur fossils were just rocks with no original animal material left inside. They believed that proteins and other organic molecules would break down completely after millions of years.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Liverpool studied a well-preserved fossil of an Edmontosaurus, a duck-billed dinosaur that lived 66 million years ago. Using advanced tools like mass spectrometry and protein sequencing, they found traces of collagen, a key protein in bones, inside the fossil. They also confirmed the presence of hydroxyproline, an amino acid linked to collagen, proving these proteins are original and not contamination.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that some dinosaur fossils still hold original biological molecules. This could change how scientists study dinosaurs, allowing them to learn more about how these animals lived, grew, and evolved by examining ancient proteins. It also suggests that old fossil collections might have hidden molecular information waiting to be explored.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to re-examine other fossils using similar methods to find more preserved proteins. They will also investigate how these molecules survived for so long, possibly due to special conditions inside the bones that protect them from decay. This could open new research paths in paleontology and evolutionary biology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found real dinosaur proteins inside 66-million-year-old bones, offering a new way to study ancient life beyond just fossils’ shapes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des protéines anciennes dans des os de dinosaures vieux de 66 millions d'années
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, les scientifiques pensaient que les fossiles de dinosaures n’étaient que des roches sans aucun matériau animal original. Ils croyaient que les protéines et autres molécules organiques disparaissaient complètement après des millions d’années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Liverpool ont étudié un fossile bien conservé d’Edmontosaurus, un dinosaure à bec de canard qui a vécu il y a 66 millions d’années. Grâce à des outils avancés comme la spectrométrie de masse et le séquençage des protéines, ils ont trouvé des traces de collagène, une protéine importante dans les os, à l’intérieur du fossile. Ils ont aussi confirmé la présence d’hydroxyproline, un acide aminé lié au collagène, prouvant que ces protéines sont originales et non une contamination.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que certains fossiles de dinosaures contiennent encore des molécules biologiques originales. Cela pourrait changer la façon dont les scientifiques étudient les dinosaures, en leur permettant d’en apprendre davantage sur leur vie, leur croissance et leur évolution en examinant ces protéines anciennes. Cela suggère aussi que les collections de fossiles anciennes pourraient cacher des informations moléculaires à découvrir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient de réexaminer d’autres fossiles avec des méthodes similaires pour trouver plus de protéines préservées. Ils chercheront aussi à comprendre comment ces molécules ont survécu si longtemps, peut-être grâce à des conditions spéciales à l’intérieur des os qui les protègent de la décomposition. Cela pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles voies de recherche en paléontologie et biologie évolutive.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé de véritables protéines de dinosaures dans des os vieux de 66 millions d’années, offrant une nouvelle façon d’étudier la vie ancienne au-delà de la forme des fossiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran proteínas antiguas dentro de huesos de dinosaurios de 66 millones de años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que los fósiles de dinosaurios eran solo rocas sin material animal original. Creían que las proteínas y otras moléculas orgánicas se descomponían completamente después de millones de años.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Liverpool estudiaron un fósil bien conservado de un Edmontosaurus, un dinosaurio con pico de pato que vivió hace 66 millones de años. Usando herramientas avanzadas como espectrometría de masas y secuenciación de proteínas, encontraron rastros de colágeno, una proteína clave en los huesos, dentro del fósil. También confirmaron la presencia de hidroxiprolina, un aminoácido ligado al colágeno, demostrando que estas proteínas son originales y no contaminación.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que algunos fósiles de dinosaurios todavía contienen moléculas biológicas originales. Esto podría cambiar la forma en que los científicos estudian a los dinosaurios, permitiéndoles aprender más sobre cómo vivían, crecían y evolucionaban al examinar estas proteínas antiguas. También sugiere que colecciones antiguas de fósiles podrían tener información molecular oculta lista para explorarse.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean revisar otros fósiles usando métodos similares para encontrar más proteínas preservadas. También investigarán cómo estas moléculas sobrevivieron tanto tiempo, posiblemente debido a condiciones especiales dentro de los huesos que las protegen de la descomposición. Esto podría abrir nuevas vías de investigación en paleontología y biología evolutiva.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos encontraron proteínas reales de dinosaurios dentro de huesos de 66 millones de años, ofreciendo una nueva forma de estudiar la vida antigua más allá de la forma de los fósiles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a partially fossilized Edmontosaurus hip bone embedded in soft earth, with delicate glowing strands of collagen-like fibers subtly intertwined within the bone’s texture, set against a calm prehistoric landscape bathed in gentle natural tones.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden math secret inside Chinese money plant leaves
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a hidden mathematical secret inside the leaves of the Chinese money plant: a naturally occurring geometric pattern known as a Voronoi diagram, something typically associated with city planning, computer science, and network design. By mapping tiny pores and looping veins in the plant’s leaves, researchers discovered that the plant organizes itself using the same kind of elegant spatial logic humans use to solve complex distance problems — without ever “measuring” anything.
articles.content:
People often spot familiar shapes in random places. Maybe you have looked at the clouds and imagined a sailboat, a seahorse, or even your great-aunt Rosemary staring back at you. Scientists call this tendency to find meaningful patterns in randomness "apophenia." But in some cases, those patterns are very real. Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Associate Professor Saket Navlakha studies the hidden structures that appear throughout nature.

One of the best known examples of organized patterning is the Voronoi diagram, a geometric system that divides space into separate regions around central points. A simple example would be school districts. Each district (region) is arranged so students are always closest to the school (central point) assigned to them.

"Voronoi diagrams have been used for centuries in a variety of applications ranging from city planning to network design," Navlakha says.

Patterns resembling Voronoi diagrams can often be seen in nature, including the markings on giraffes. However, these natural versions usually do not contain the obvious central points found in textbook examples. Navlakha and former graduate student Cici Zheng recently identified a rare exception in Pilea peperomioides, better known as the Chinese money plant.

Chinese Money Plant Reveals Hidden Mathematical Pattern

The Chinese money plant is a perennial species native to China's Yunnan and Sichuan provinces. It is also a popular houseplant often given as a gift. Its circular leaves contain noticeable pores called hydathodes, which are surrounded by looping vein networks that move water and nutrients through the leaf.

After carefully mapping the pores and veins, Navlakha and Zheng found that the leaf structure naturally forms a Voronoi pattern.

To better understand how the pattern develops, the researchers partnered with Przemysław Prusinkiewicz, a scientist internationally recognized for his work on plant vein formation. Together, they identified the "natural algorithm" responsible for creating the looping veins around the pores in the leaves.

"Just as humans have to solve problems to survive, the same goes for other organisms," says Zheng, now a postdoc at the Allen Institute. "But unlike humans, plants cannot explicitly measure distances! Instead, they rely on local biological interactions to achieve the same Voronoi solution."

Hidden Algorithms in Nature

The discovery highlights how living organisms can create highly organized systems without conscious planning or measurement.

"We think of these algorithms in nature as an explanation for how organisms will behave and as a way to try to make sense of the world," Navlakha says. "This example is a nice merger of classical geometry, modern plant biology, and computer science."

Prusinkiewicz says the findings may finally answer a long-standing scientific mystery involving leaf vein formation.

"It's remarkable how mathematical yet another aspect of plant form and patterning turns out to be," Prusinkiewicz adds. "For decades, the question of how reticulate veins form has remained open, and finally we have a plausible answer" in Chinese money plants' Voronoi patterns.

Navlakha and Zheng hope future studies of these patterns will reveal more about how plants solve complicated biological challenges. They believe the work could eventually help scientists better understand the mathematical principles that shape evolution, development, and life itself.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing a natural mathematical pattern (Voronoi diagram) in Chinese money plant leaves, which advances understanding of plant biology and natural algorithms. This breakthrough has broad implications for biology, mathematics, and computer science, offering insights into natural problem-solving and potentially influencing future research in evolution and development.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover natural math pattern in Chinese money plant leaves
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long noticed patterns in nature that resemble human-made designs, but understanding how these patterns form without planning or measuring has been a challenge. One such pattern is the Voronoi diagram, a way to divide space around points, commonly used in city planning and computer science.

**What happened** – Researchers at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory studied the leaves of the Chinese money plant, a popular houseplant with round leaves full of tiny pores and veins. They mapped these pores and veins and discovered that the plant naturally creates a Voronoi pattern. This means the plant’s leaf veins loop around pores in a way that matches a complex geometric system usually made by humans. The team worked with a plant vein expert to identify the biological process, or “natural algorithm,” behind this pattern.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows plants can solve complex spatial problems without measuring distances like humans do. Instead, they rely on local biological signals to form these organized patterns. This finding solves a long-standing mystery about how leaf veins develop and connects math, biology, and computer science in a new way. It helps scientists understand how nature uses simple rules to create complex designs.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to study these natural algorithms further to learn how plants solve other biological problems. This could help scientists understand the mathematical rules behind how plants grow and evolve, possibly leading to new discoveries in biology and even inspiring new computer algorithms.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found that Chinese money plant leaves naturally form a complex math pattern called a Voronoi diagram, revealing how plants solve spatial problems without measuring.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un motif mathématique naturel dans les feuilles du pilea
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont souvent remarqué des motifs dans la nature qui ressemblent à des dessins humains, mais comprendre comment ces motifs se forment sans plan ni mesure restait difficile. Un de ces motifs est le diagramme de Voronoï, une façon de diviser l'espace autour de points, utilisée en urbanisme et en informatique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory ont étudié les feuilles du pilea, une plante d’intérieur aux feuilles rondes avec de petits pores et des veines. Ils ont cartographié ces pores et veines et découvert que la plante crée naturellement un motif de Voronoï. Cela signifie que les veines forment des boucles autour des pores selon un système géométrique complexe habituellement créé par les humains. L’équipe a travaillé avec un expert en formation de veines végétales pour identifier le « programme naturel » responsable.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les plantes peuvent résoudre des problèmes spatiaux complexes sans mesurer les distances comme les humains. Elles utilisent plutôt des signaux biologiques locaux pour créer ces motifs organisés. Cela résout un mystère ancien sur la formation des veines des feuilles et relie mathématiques, biologie et informatique d’une nouvelle façon. Cela aide à comprendre comment la nature utilise des règles simples pour créer des dessins complexes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier ces algorithmes naturels pour comprendre comment les plantes résolvent d’autres problèmes biologiques. Cela pourrait aider à découvrir les règles mathématiques qui gouvernent la croissance et l’évolution des plantes, et inspirer de nouveaux algorithmes informatiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que les feuilles du pilea forment naturellement un motif mathématique complexe appelé diagramme de Voronoï, montrant comment les plantes résolvent des problèmes spatiaux sans mesurer.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren patrón matemático natural en las hojas de la planta del dinero china
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han notado patrones en la naturaleza que se parecen a diseños hechos por humanos, pero entender cómo se forman sin planificación ni medición ha sido difícil. Uno de estos patrones es el diagrama de Voronoi, una forma de dividir el espacio alrededor de puntos, usado en planificación urbana y ciencias de la computación.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory estudiaron las hojas de la planta del dinero china, una planta de interior con hojas redondas llenas de pequeños poros y venas. Al mapear estos poros y venas, descubrieron que la planta crea naturalmente un patrón de Voronoi. Esto significa que las venas forman bucles alrededor de los poros siguiendo un sistema geométrico complejo que normalmente hacen los humanos. El equipo trabajó con un experto en formación de venas para identificar el «algoritmo natural» detrás de este patrón.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que las plantas pueden resolver problemas espaciales complejos sin medir distancias como los humanos. En cambio, usan señales biológicas locales para formar estos patrones organizados. Esto resuelve un misterio antiguo sobre cómo se forman las venas de las hojas y conecta las matemáticas, la biología y la informática de una forma nueva. Ayuda a entender cómo la naturaleza usa reglas simples para crear diseños complejos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar estos algoritmos naturales para aprender cómo las plantas resuelven otros problemas biológicos. Esto podría ayudar a entender las reglas matemáticas que guían el crecimiento y la evolución de las plantas, e inspirar nuevos algoritmos computacionales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron que las hojas de la planta del dinero china forman naturalmente un patrón matemático complejo llamado diagrama de Voronoi, mostrando cómo las plantas resuelven problemas espaciales sin medir.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a single Chinese money plant leaf viewed close-up, its circular shape filled with softly glowing, interconnected pores surrounded by delicate looping veins forming a precise Voronoi pattern, rendered in gentle natural greens and earthy tones against a simple, light background.

articles.title: 'Millions' of pounds saved by replacing Palantir tech in refugee system
articles.summary: The government said its in-house IT system was "more flexible" while meeting "high standards" of security.
articles.content: Rob Miller, of Public Digital - a consultancy founded by former government tech experts - added that the government should not just consider whether to curb reliance on big tech "but how quickly it is willing to invest in the capability to do so."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a government initiative to replace expensive third-party technology with a more flexible, secure in-house IT system for refugee management, resulting in significant cost savings. This represents a meaningful improvement in public sector technology with potential broad benefits for government efficiency and refugee services.
articles.personality_title: UK saves millions by replacing Palantir tech with new refugee system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The UK government has been using technology from Palantir, a private company, to manage information about refugees. This system was costly and relied on outside help.

**What happened** – The government built its own IT system to replace Palantir’s technology. This new system is run internally, meaning government employees control it directly. It was designed to be flexible and secure, meeting high safety standards. Experts from Public Digital, a group of former government technology workers, supported this change.

**Impact** – By switching to the new system, the government saved millions of pounds. The new technology is not only cheaper but also more adaptable to changing needs. This helps the government manage refugee information better and more efficiently.

**What's next step** – The government may continue to develop its own technology for other public services, reducing reliance on big tech companies. Experts suggest investing more in building these internal abilities quickly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK government’s new in-house refugee system saves millions and offers a flexible, secure way to handle important data without depending on expensive outside technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni économise des millions en remplaçant la technologie Palantir dans le système des réfugiés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le gouvernement britannique utilisait la technologie de Palantir, une entreprise privée, pour gérer les informations sur les réfugiés. Ce système était coûteux et dépendait d'une aide extérieure.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le gouvernement a créé son propre système informatique pour remplacer la technologie de Palantir. Ce nouveau système est géré en interne, ce qui signifie que ce sont des employés du gouvernement qui le contrôlent directement. Il a été conçu pour être flexible et sécurisé, respectant des normes élevées de sécurité. Des experts de Public Digital, un groupe d'anciens techniciens gouvernementaux, ont soutenu ce changement.

**Impact** – En passant au nouveau système, le gouvernement a économisé des millions de livres. La nouvelle technologie est non seulement moins chère, mais aussi plus adaptable aux besoins changeants. Cela aide le gouvernement à mieux gérer les informations sur les réfugiés et à être plus efficace.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement pourrait continuer à développer ses propres technologies pour d'autres services publics, réduisant ainsi sa dépendance aux grandes entreprises technologiques. Les experts suggèrent d'investir rapidement dans le développement de ces capacités internes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau système interne du gouvernement britannique pour les réfugiés permet d’économiser des millions tout en offrant une gestion flexible et sécurisée des données importantes, sans dépendre d’une technologie coûteuse externe.
articles.personality_title_es: Reino Unido ahorra millones al reemplazar la tecnología de Palantir en el sistema de refugiados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El gobierno del Reino Unido usaba tecnología de Palantir, una empresa privada, para manejar información sobre refugiados. Este sistema era caro y dependía de ayuda externa.

**Qué pasó** – El gobierno creó su propio sistema informático para reemplazar la tecnología de Palantir. Este nuevo sistema es manejado internamente, es decir, por empleados del gobierno. Fue diseñado para ser flexible y seguro, cumpliendo con altos estándares de protección. Expertos de Public Digital, un grupo de antiguos trabajadores tecnológicos del gobierno, apoyaron este cambio.

**Impacto** – Al cambiar al nuevo sistema, el gobierno ahorró millones de libras. La nueva tecnología es más barata y se adapta mejor a las necesidades cambiantes. Esto ayuda a que el gobierno gestione mejor la información sobre refugiados y sea más eficiente.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno podría seguir desarrollando su propia tecnología para otros servicios públicos, reduciendo la dependencia de grandes empresas tecnológicas. Los expertos sugieren invertir rápidamente en fortalecer estas capacidades internas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo sistema interno del gobierno británico para refugiados ahorra millones y ofrece una forma flexible y segura de manejar datos importantes sin depender de tecnología externa costosa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sturdy, open wooden bridge symbolizing new pathways, spanning over calm waters with softly glowing lanterns representing saved resources, while abstract silhouettes of interconnected gears and digital nodes gently transform into simple, hand-crafted tools, illustrating the shift from complex big tech to human-centered solutions in the refugee system.

articles.title: 3 things you need to know about quantum computers, from an expert
articles.summary: What use is a quantum computer? Perhaps both more and less than you think, according to quantum computing expert Shayan Majidy
articles.content:
What use is a quantum computer? Perhaps both more and less than you think, according to quantum computing expert Shayan Majidy

Quantum computers are strange-looking machines ROBERT GHEMENT/EPA/Shutterstock

Picture a quantum computer. Are you imagining an ordinary computer, but somehow just better? If so, that would be a mistake, because quantum computers are fundamentally different. They rely on exotic quantum phenomena occurring between their constituent parts, known as qubits, but their strange nature often invites myths and misconceptions. Quantum computing expert Shayan Majidy at Harvard University, the lead author of Building Quantum Computers, is here to get you up to speed.

1. Quantum computers already exist

I was just on a flight and another passenger asked me: “When we will we actually have quantum computers?” But they do exist and we use them daily! Scientists all over the world are using them, and some companies have even made them publicly available so people sitting in their homes can get access to quantum computers and use them.

Having said that, quantum computers are not yet similar to something like large language models, where you just open your laptop and use it all the time. They are still more specialized devices and there is a spectrum in how people use them. Experimentalists who are working on bettering quantum computers are working on them every day. In fact, many researchers are using quantum computers to construct the building blocks for future large-scale quantum computers. Or we’re using them to probe very fundamental science questions.

We are at the crest of a wave of demonstrating how to use quantum computers for things that conventional computers can’t do. I wouldn’t be surprised if within five, 10 years, I mentor students who are just routinely accessing quantum computers through the cloud, always running some experiment like that.

2. Quantum computers will not make every computation easier

The misconception here is that quantum computers are just simply going to be better, faster computers, and they will make classical computers obsolete. But quantum computers are not generically faster – I like to describe them as differently capable instead. This means that they offer meaningful speed-ups only for very specific problems.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Two famous examples of such problems are factoring large numbers faster than any known classical algorithm, which is important for breaking encryption, and also faster searching through unstructured data. Additionally, there are problems like simulating quantum systems, sampling tasks, certain optimization problems, and some linear algebra problems under very specific assumptions.

So, the advantage of quantum computers doesn’t come from raw speed, but it comes from very surgically designed quantum algorithms. These algorithms exploit superposition, interference, entanglement, all these inherently quantum effects, and using them happens to suit a very narrow class of problems.

But outside of these cases, pretty much for anything like browsing the web, sending text messages, playing games, a quantum computer doesn’t offer any advantage beyond what the laptop can do. There’s a class of problems that we think are quantumly easy and classically hard, and these are the problems that we’d want to solve with a quantum computer. The ones that are classically easy, they’re already classically easy, so let’s not use our quantum computers on them, it would just be a huge waste of time and resources.

3. A quantum computer is not equivalent to lots of classical computers working simultaneously

I find that people often have this picture in mind that quantum computers try every calculation at once, because their qubits can be put into special superposition states, and that’s why they’re generically more powerful. A superposition state does mean that a qubit exists in a combination of zero and one at once. And it is also true that if you have n qubits, their quantum state will be described by exponentially many, or 2n options. But the myth of infinite parallelism breaks down because you can’t actually read that information out. You can’t actually read out exponentially many answers. The moment you go and measure the qubits’ state, it collapses into a single, ordinary, classical value.

So, the real story is much more subtle. The quantum computer can give many answers, and we engineer algorithms to amplify the correct ones, and suppress the wrong ones. The really good algorithms take all these superposed options and make it so that when the final measurement occurs, the right answer is the one that emerges.
articles.reasoning: The article provides a clear, expert explanation of quantum computers, highlighting their current existence, specialized capabilities, and potential future impact. It educates the public on a complex technological breakthrough that could have broad implications for science and computing, making it inspiring and informative.
articles.personality_title: Quantum computers: what they are and how they really work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are a new kind of machine that work very differently from regular computers. They use special parts called qubits and strange quantum rules that can seem confusing. Many people have questions about what quantum computers do and why they matter.

**What happened** – Shayan Majidy, a quantum computing expert from Harvard, explains three key facts: quantum computers already exist and are used by scientists worldwide; they don’t make every task faster but help solve specific problems better; and they don’t work by trying every answer at once, but by carefully using quantum effects to find the right solution.

**Impact** – This clear explanation helps people understand that quantum computers are not just faster laptops but unique tools designed for certain challenges, like breaking codes or simulating molecules. Knowing this helps avoid myths and shows how quantum computers could change science and technology in the future.

**What's next step** – Researchers continue to improve quantum computers and create new algorithms. In the next 5 to 10 years, more students and scientists may use quantum computers through the internet to run experiments and solve problems that regular computers cannot handle.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Quantum computers are special machines already in use that solve certain complex problems in new ways, offering different abilities rather than just more speed.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les ordinateurs quantiques : ce qu’ils sont et comment ils fonctionnent vraiment
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont un nouveau type de machines qui fonctionnent très différemment des ordinateurs classiques. Ils utilisent des parties spéciales appelées qubits et des règles quantiques étranges qui peuvent sembler compliquées. Beaucoup de gens se demandent à quoi servent vraiment ces ordinateurs et pourquoi ils sont importants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Shayan Majidy, expert en informatique quantique à Harvard, explique trois faits clés : les ordinateurs quantiques existent déjà et sont utilisés par des scientifiques dans le monde entier ; ils ne rendent pas toutes les tâches plus rapides mais aident à résoudre certains problèmes spécifiques ; et ils ne fonctionnent pas en essayant toutes les réponses en même temps, mais en utilisant soigneusement les effets quantiques pour trouver la bonne solution.

**Impact** – Cette explication claire aide à comprendre que les ordinateurs quantiques ne sont pas de simples ordinateurs plus rapides, mais des outils uniques conçus pour certains défis, comme casser des codes ou simuler des molécules. Cela permet d’éviter les idées fausses et montre comment ces ordinateurs pourraient transformer la science et la technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continuent d’améliorer les ordinateurs quantiques et de créer de nouveaux algorithmes. Dans 5 à 10 ans, plus d’étudiants et de scientifiques pourraient utiliser ces ordinateurs via Internet pour réaliser des expériences et résoudre des problèmes que les ordinateurs classiques ne peuvent pas gérer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines spéciales déjà utilisées qui résolvent certains problèmes complexes de nouvelles façons, offrant des capacités différentes plutôt qu’une simple rapidité accrue.
articles.personality_title_es: Computadoras cuánticas: qué son y cómo funcionan realmente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son un tipo nuevo de máquina que funcionan muy diferente a las computadoras normales. Usan partes especiales llamadas qubits y reglas cuánticas extrañas que pueden parecer difíciles de entender. Muchas personas se preguntan para qué sirven y por qué son importantes.

**Qué pasó** – Shayan Majidy, experto en computación cuántica de Harvard, explica tres puntos clave: las computadoras cuánticas ya existen y las usan científicos en todo el mundo; no hacen que todas las tareas sean más rápidas, pero ayudan a resolver problemas específicos; y no funcionan probando todas las respuestas a la vez, sino usando efectos cuánticos para encontrar la solución correcta.

**Impacto** – Esta explicación clara ayuda a entender que las computadoras cuánticas no son solo computadoras más rápidas, sino herramientas únicas para ciertos retos, como romper códigos o simular moléculas. Esto ayuda a evitar ideas equivocadas y muestra cómo podrían cambiar la ciencia y la tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores siguen mejorando las computadoras cuánticas y creando nuevos algoritmos. En los próximos 5 a 10 años, más estudiantes y científicos podrían usar estas computadoras por internet para hacer experimentos y resolver problemas que las computadoras normales no pueden.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas especiales ya en uso que resuelven ciertos problemas complejos de maneras nuevas, ofreciendo habilidades diferentes en lugar de solo más velocidad.
